questions.prompt: What is the difference between `cpcmd common:get-load` and `cpcmd -d ablemoose.example common:get-load`?
choices: They are identical; -d is decorative
The first runs in admin context (server load); the second runs in Able Moose's account context (the account's reported load via their available counters)
The first reads from the panel; the second reads from the CLI
The first is synchronous; the second is asynchronous
questions.explanation: -d sets the call's authentication context to the named site. The same module:function executes against different scopes depending on whether -d is set.

questions.prompt: The Login-As button in Nexus is a UI wrapper for the admin:hijack API call.
questions.explanation: admin:hijack($site, $user, $gate) is the backend call. Nexus's button invokes it; cpcmd admin:hijack does the same; SOAP API consumers do the same. One backing call, three rendering surfaces.

questions.prompt: Why is hand-editing /usr/local/apnscp/config/config.ini wrong even if the resulting state looks correct?
choices: Config.ini is encrypted; hand-edits corrupt it
Setting a Scope triggers a cascade (re-render dependent config, restart services); a hand-edit doesn't, leaving partial state, and the next Bootstrapper run reverts the edit
ApisCP licensing forbids hand-edits to config files
Config.ini is symlinked to a remote file; local edits are not persistent
questions.explanation: Scopes are callable; they trigger configuration cascades and service restarts that hand-edits don't. The Bootstrapper is idempotent and reverts drift, so hand-edits also don't survive the next platform update. Use cpcmd scope:set or cp.config:set.

questions.prompt: Which of these are independent default-on security subsystems in an ApisCP install? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Rampart (jail-based brute-force deterrent)
Shield (Apache-layer DOS handler)
ModSecurity (request-body inspection with OWASP CRS)
ClamAV (malware scanning)
Fortification (filesystem ACL privilege separation)
An nftables ruleset that blocks all non-residential IPs
questions.explanation: Rampart, Shield, ModSec, ClamAV, and Fortification all run by default; the nftables blanket block is fabricated. Fortification is an isolation layer rather than an attack-detection layer, but it's part of the default-on security posture.

questions.prompt: MSP helpdesk techs are getting locked out of customer accounts from the office IP whenever they typo a password. What's the right fix?
choices: Raise the Rampart failure threshold platform-wide
Whitelist the MSP office IP with `cpcmd rampart:whitelist <ip>`
Tell the techs to use a different IP
Turn off Rampart entirely
questions.explanation: Whitelist the trusted source IPs (office, RMM/PSA outbound). Don't slacken the threshold platform-wide because of a known-trusted source; that loosens protection for everyone else.

questions.prompt: Under Fortification MIN on a WordPress install, the apache user has which set of permissions to /var/www/wp/wp-config.php?
choices: Read-write, the same as the account user
Read-only (apache can read it for the DB credentials but can't modify it)
No access at all (only the account user can read it)
Execute-only
questions.explanation: PHP needs to read wp-config.php to know the DB credentials, so apache has read. PHP shouldn't be able to overwrite the file (RCE → backdoor would write here), so apache lacks write. Read-only ACL captures both constraints.

questions.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for a runaway-process triage on a customer's account.
items: Freeze the account's cgroup so processes pause without dying
Read `ps -u <account-user>` and the access log to identify the offending process
Thaw the cgroup so the rest of the account resumes
questions.explanation: Freeze first (so the picture stops moving while you investigate), identify the offender, kill that specific process, thaw to resume the rest. Kill-then-investigate kills more than it should.

questions.prompt: Which of these paths MUST be in a complete ApisCP account backup? (Select all that apply.)
choices: /home/virtual/site*/shadow
/home/virtual/site*/info
/var/log/mailer_table*
/var/lib/mysql/mysql-grants*
/var/lib/mysql (the live MySQL data files)
/var/lib/pgsql/*/backups
questions.explanation: Shadow (data), info (metadata), mailer_table (mailbox mapping), mysql-grants and pgsql backups directory (the logical exports). The live /var/lib/mysql directory is NOT to be backed up directly; it produces inconsistent snapshots. Use the logical exports written nightly by backup_dbs.php.

questions.prompt: An MSP wants to model a sub-reseller relationship. Which field set creates the master/subordinate hierarchy ApisCP exposes?
choices: siteinfo,parent on each subordinate, pointing to the master's domain
billing,invoice=<id> on the master, and billing,parent_invoice=<same-id> on each subordinate
users,parent=<master-user> on each subordinate account
Create a custom Reseller Scope and write each subordinate's parent into it
questions.explanation: billing,invoice on the master + billing,parent_invoice on each subordinate is the canonical pattern. Suspend/Activate/Delete operations accept the master's invoice ID and apply across the group; the same field is the join key for billing system integration.

questions.prompt: What's the cleanest cpcmd call to get every account's primary domain plus their currently-applied plan?
choices: cpcmd admin:list-accounts
cpcmd admin:collect '[siteinfo.domain,siteinfo.plan]'
cpcmd webapp:list-sites
cpcmd misc:list-accounts --plan
questions.explanation: admin:collect is the bulk-reader. First argument is the fields to read (dot-notation through the service.parameter shape); optional second argument is a filter. Returns a map of site -> requested fields.

questions.prompt: A Web App left in the unfortify state long-term has the same attack surface as a normal hosting panel with no privilege separation.
questions.explanation: Unfortify is a one-shot escape hatch for maintenance. Leaving it on means a compromised PHP process can write anywhere in the account, including core files; the privilege separation Fortification gives you is gone.

questions.prompt: A 4-core ApisCP server is on the default `system.watchdog-load` of 50. What's the right operational response?
choices: Leave it; the default works on every server
Lower the threshold (e.g. to 8 or 10) so the watchdog reacts before the small server is saturated
Raise the threshold so the watchdog never intervenes
Disable the watchdog entirely on small servers
questions.explanation: Load-average thresholds scale with core count. A load of 50 on a 32-core server is workable; on a 4-core server it's catastrophic. The right operational move is to tune the threshold to the actual hardware; a rough heuristic is 1-2× the core count.
